segments.s428-g161: برآمد is a third-person past/epic verb meaning 'he arose/appeared/issued forth' (here 'he emerged'). چنان modifies the following adjective خسته to mean 'so/so very' (چنان خسته = 'so tired'). زان is the contracted poetic form of از + آن, 'from that', and governs آبگیر, the place he comes out of; word order is Verb + degree + adjective + prepositional phrase, with the subject omitted and understood from context.
segments.s428-g162: سراسر precedes تن and means 'throughout'/'all over', so سراسر تنش = 'his whole body' (تن + possessive suffix ش = 'his body'). پر ز is the idiom 'full of' (پر = 'full (of)'; ز = 'of/from') and takes the following nouns; پیکان تیر are two synonymous nouns for projectiles ('arrows'), here used together for emphasis. The phrase as a whole describes his body being filled with arrows.
segments.s428-g163: برآنم is a fixed first-person present meaning 'I am resolved/I intend'. که introduces the subordinate clause that follows. چون functions as a temporal conjunction 'when/as' here. او به ایوان رسد is a clause with رسد in the present/subjunctive form used to express a future or potential action ('he reaches/arrives at the iwan'); the subject 'he' is the pronoun او.
segments.s428-g164: روانش = روان + possessive ش, 'his soul/spirit', which functions as the subject of the following verb. ز ایوان ('from the iwan') marks origin, and به کیوان رسد ('reaches Kayvān') states the destination; رسد is parallel to the previous verb and here expresses the soul's movement toward Kayvān (the planet/name used metaphorically). The clause is succinctly telegraphic as in epic poetry.
segments.s428-g73: مگر here functions as a conditional/exceptive 'unless'. دادگر is a noun meaning 'a just one, just ruler or judge' placed before the verb for emphasis. باشدت is an old/subjunctive/optative form meaning 'may there be / be to you' (here introducing the wished-for state) with the sense directed toward 'you'. رهنمای is the noun 'guide' that is the predicate complement of باشدت.
segments.s428-g74: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'. بیرون شوی is a second‑person present/subjunctive verbal phrase 'you go out / you become outside' (شوی = 'become/go' in classical usage). زین here introduces the following noun phrase but, in this edition's provided senses, its usual meanings don't fit precisely (see senses). سپنجی is an adjective 'spongy / sponge‑like' modifying سرای. سرای is the head noun 'dwelling/house' (the sense list given in the tokens does not include this common meaning).
segments.s428-g75: چنین گفت رستم is the main clause 'thus said Rostam' (چنین = 'thus'). که introduces a following clause describing Rostam. بیگاه شد is a past/perfective clause: بیگاه 'untimely/abrupt' + شد 'became/was' — together meaning 'he was suddenly/untimely struck' or 'was taken unawares'.
segments.s428-g76: ز رزم و ز بد دست کوتاه شد is a causal/cartographic phrase: repeated preposition ز 'from/because of' marks causes ('from battle and from ill‑fortune'). بد دست is a compound meaning 'of bad hand' i.e. 'ill‑favored / unlucky' and دست here means 'power/control'. کوتاه شد is 'became short' used idiomatically to mean 'became reduced / was deprived' — hence 'became powerless'.
segments.s428-g77: شب تیره is a noun phrase 'dark night' (adjective follows noun in poetry). هرگز 'never' negates the verb. که here links to the verb clause and can be read as a relative/connector. جوید نبرد literally 'seeks battle' (جوید = 'seeks/searches for'), so the clause states that a dark night never seeks battle.
segments.s428-g78: تو اکنون بدین رامشی بازگرد is an imperative addressed to 'تو' (you). اکنون 'now' sets time. بدین is the classical/poetic prepositional phrase 'to/into this' (written here as ب + دین in the tokens), followed by رامشی 'this calm/repose' as object. بازگرد is the imperative 'return (to)'.
segments.s428-g79: من اکنون چنین سوی ایوان شوم places the subject 'من' (I) and time 'اکنون' (now) up front. چنین 'thus' qualifies the action. سوی ایوان 'toward the iwan/porch' gives direction. شوم is a first‑person subjunctive/optative (classical jussive) 'may I become / may I go' used as a future/intention marker ('I will go' / 'let me go').
segments.s428-g80: بیاسایم و یک زمان بغنوم consists of two jussive verbs expressing wish/intention: بیاسایم 'may I rest' and بغنوم 'may I sleep/slumber'. یک زمان 'for a short while / one moment' modifies بغنوم to indicate duration. The conjunction و simply links the two verbs.
segments.s428-g89: A verbless predication where تو (you) is the subject and مردی بزرگی is the predicate: Persian often omits the copula (هستی). مردی is a noun ('man') with the following بزرگی expressing the quality 'greatness' by juxtaposition rather than an explicit adjective. زور آزمای is a compound: زور 'strength' + آزمای (the stem/participial form of آزما) meaning 'one who tests' or 'tester' — together 'tester/challenger of strength.'
segments.s428-g90: بسی is an adverb meaning 'much' / 'many'; it modifies the following noun phrase. چاره دانی is a nominal + a verb (دانی, 2nd-singular present of دانستن) meaning 'you know remedies' — the verb agrees with the addressed تو. The coordinating و links additional nouns (نیرنگ و رای) that are understood as also known by the same verb (ellipsis of a repeated verb): 'and (you know) stratagems and counsels.'
segments.s428-g91: بدیدم is the past tense 'I saw' (verb before its objects, a common poetic order). همه ('all') quantifies the coordinated nouns فر ('(royal) glory') و زیب ('beauty'). The enclitic ت (second-person suffix) attached to زیب marks possession ('your beauty'), and the object marker را follows, making the whole phrase the definite object of بدیدم — conventionally both coordinated nouns are taken as referring to the addressee.
segments.s428-g92: نخواهم is the first-person negative of خواستن ('I do not/will not want'); it introduces a subordinate clause with که ('that'). بینم here is the present-subjunctive form of دیدن used after نخواهم که, meaning 'that I see' (classical Persian uses the present-stem form). نشیب ترا ('your descent/decline') is the object of بینم, with ت as the 2nd-person suffix and را as the definite/object marker.
segments.s428-g93: This clause opens with the oath-like prepositional phrase 'به جان' (literally 'by/on [my] life') used to strengthen the speaker's statement. 'امشبی' is a temporal adverb formed from 'امشب' + the suffix -ی meaning 'this night/tonight'. 'دادمت' is a past-tense verb with an enclitic object pronoun: 'دادم' (I gave) + '-ت' (you) = 'I gave you' (classical Persian often compresses subject/object clitics this way). 'زینهار' is a fixed admonitory interjection meaning 'beware' or 'take heed' and follows the verbal clause as a warning addressing the listener.
segments.s428-g94: 'به ایوان رسی' is a temporal/conditional clause headed by the preposition 'به' (here 'towards/into') plus 'ایوان' (portico) and the verb 'رسی' (2nd-person singular of رسید — 'you reach/attain'); together it means 'when/if you reach the portico'. 'کام کژی' is a noun phrase 'crooked desire' (کام = desire/wish, کژی = crookedness/perversity) functioning as the semantic object. 'مخار' is the final verbal/particle element that forbids or negates the action; its precise lexical sense is uncertain in the provided lexicon, but here it functions as a prohibition equivalent to 'do not [take/seek/indulge in]'. Word order is typical of classical Persian: temporal subordinate clause → object phrase → prohibitive particle/verb.
segments.s428-g95: سخن هرچ پذرفتی is a relative clause with the noun سخن 'words/speech' fronted, هرچ (هر چه) meaning 'whatever', and پذرفتی the simple past second-person 'you accepted/agreed'. آن را بکن places the demonstrative آن 'that' before the object marker را and the imperative بکن 'do (it)'; together it means 'do that'. Note the classical/poetic ordering: relative clause → demonstrative + را → imperative.
segments.s428-g96: ازین پس is the set phrase 'از این پس' meaning 'from now on / henceforth' (از = from, ین = این orthographic variant, پس = after/from now on). مپیمای is a negative-form present-subjunctive/imperative (م- + پیمای) addressed to 'you' meaning 'do not attempt/try/measure (here: try to speak)'; Ferdowsi uses م- for negation in imperatives. با من سخن is a prepositional phrase 'with me + speech', i.e. 'speak with me' — together: 'do not attempt to speak with me (from now on)'.
segments.s428-g113: شگفتی بمانده is literally “astonishment/remonstration has remained”: بمانده is the past participle/predicative of ماندن used as a perfective result (‘has remained’). بد before the name is an evaluative adjective meaning ‘ill/wretched’ describing اسفندیار; Persian often omits a preposition, so the phrase means “astonishment remained concerning the ill Esfandiyar.”
segments.s428-g114: همی گفت is an archaic/prosodic combination: همی gives a durative or emphatic flavor to گفت (past ‘said’), i.e. ‘he kept saying’ or ‘he was saying’. کای is a vocative particle ‘O’, followed by the address noun داور and its epithet کامگار ‘successful/victorious’; the whole is a direct vocative: “O victorious Judge.”
segments.s428-g115: چنان آفریدی که introduces a result/relational clause: چنان ‘thus/so’ + آفریدی (2nd‑person past of آفریدن) ‘you created’; که starts the clause that expresses the content/result. خود before خواستی is emphatic/reflexive (‘yourself’/‘as you yourself desired’) and خواستی is the 2nd‑person past of خواستن ‘you wanted/willed’.
segments.s428-g116: زمان و زمین را is a coordinated direct object (را marks definiteness/object). بیاراستی is a poetic/past 2nd‑person form of آراستن (with the prefix بی-), functioning as a transitive past tense ‘you adorned/arranged’ — here meaning you set or ordered time and earth. Word order places the object before the verb, a common Persian pattern.
segments.s428-g117: بدانگه is an adverbial (‘thereupon/at that moment’). که شد introduces a temporal/relative clause ‘when … became’. نامور ‘renowned’ is a predicate adjective and باز ‘again’ modifies it; جای (‘place’/‘rank’) is tied into the clause (either as the subject that became famed or as a locative notion—classical Persian allows this compact phrasing).
segments.s428-g118: پشوتن بیامد is straightforward past narration: the proper name پشوتن (Peshotan) is subject and بیامد is the past tense ‘came forth’. ز is the poetic preposition ‘from’ and پرده‌سرای is a compound meaning ‘curtain‑chamber’/‘behind‑the‑curtain room’ or ‘stage‑house’ — so ‘came out from the curtain‑chamber.’
segments.s428-g119: ز نوش‌آذر گرد وز مهر نوش is a compact, poetic cluster: ز introduces origin ‘from’. نوش‌آذر is a proper name (or epithet), گرد here conveys a sense of a circling/roundness or ‘a cloud/atmosphere’ associated with that source, and وز (literally ‘a blast/gust’) combines with مهر نوش (‘love‑draught’ or ‘the wine of love’) to produce the image ‘a gust/blast of love‑drink’. The phrase therefore paints a metaphoric gust/atmosphere issuing from those sources.
segments.s428-g120: خروشیدنی بود is a predicative clause: خروشیدنی is an adjective/participle ‘roaring/tumultuous’ and بود is the past copula ‘was’. با درد و جوش (‘with pain and još’) is an instrumental/qualifying phrase; درد is ‘pain/ache’ and جوش here denotes boiling/ardent surge — together forming ‘with pain and fevered agitation.’
segments.s428-g129: "که" introduces the subordinate clause ('that/as for'); "سودی نبینم" is literal 'I see no benefit' with the negated present verb نبینم placed after the noun (typical Classical Persian order). "ز خون ریختن" is a prepositional phrase using ز + the verbal noun ریختن ('the shedding/spilling of blood'), so the whole phrase means 'from the shedding of blood'.
segments.s428-g130: نشاید is an impersonal/formulaic negative ('it is not proper' / 'one should not'). What follows is an infinitival/nominal clause: "به مرگ اندر آویختن" — به + مرگ + اندر + آویختن — where مرگ is 'death' and آویختن is used in the sense 'to cling to / attach oneself (to)', so the clause means 'to cling to/throw oneself into death'. Word order puts the modal/assessment (نشاید) before the action phrase.
segments.s428-g131: "همه مرگ راایم" functions as a topical phrase 'as for death, my opinion/counsel (is)...' راایم is an older genitival/possessive expression 'my opinion' or 'my view' attached to مرگ. "برنا و پیر" simply lists 'young and old' (برنا = youthful, پیر = old) connected by و.
segments.s428-g132: "به رفتن" uses رفتن euphemistically for 'dying/passing' (here 'in dying' or 'on departing'); بادمان is an optative/formulaic construction from باد + ـمان meaning 'may ... be to us' (i.e. 'may ... be ours' / 'may ... happen for us'), and خرد دستگیر is the phrase 'wisdom [be] succoring/helpful' (دستگیر used adjectivally 'helpful, succoring'). The overall sense: 'in dying, may wisdom be our helper.'
segments.s428-g133: The phrase lists the containers: "به تابوت زرین" ('in / into a golden coffin' — به here marks the container/placement) and "در مهد ساج" ('in a cradle of saj [wood]') where در is the ordinary preposition 'in/inside' and مهد ساج is a compound 'cradle of saj (wood)'. زرین is an adjectival form 'golden'; ساج names the tree/wood.
segments.s428-g134: فرستادشان is the past transitive 'he sent them'. زی here is an archaic/poetic preposition/particle attached before the title and functions to relate the sending to the ruler (conventionally 'to/for/by the order of' the sovereign); خداوند تاج is an honorific 'the lord/sovereign of the crown' (title). The precise force of زی is archaic but it links the sending to the sovereign.
segments.s428-g135: پیامی here is an indefinite object 'a message' (پیامی فرستاد = 'he sent a message'). فرستاد is the simple past 'he sent'. نزد پدر literally 'to/into the presence of the father' (نزد used with a person means 'to' or 'before' that person).
segments.s428-g136: که introduces the content clause ('that'); شاخ رای تو is a genitival phrase 'that branch of your counsel/plan' with the possessor تو following رای (typical Persian order). آمد به بر is an idiomatic sequence: آمد = 'came' and به بر ('upon/onto') here gives the sense 'fell upon/arrived to (someone)' — so 'that branch of your counsel came upon him' / 'came to pass for him'.
segments.s428-g17: This is a compact, slightly inverted clause: the locative phrase 'بر رخش' ('on/upon Rakhsh') comes first, followed by the source phrase 'ازان تیرها' ('from those arrows'), then the verb 'گشت' (past of گشتن, 'became') and the predicate adjective 'سست' ('weak'). 'ازان' is an archaic/poetic form of modern از آن. The overall meaning is 'on Rakhsh, from those arrows, (he) became weak.'
segments.s428-g18: 'نبد' is the negative past existential ('was not' / 'there was not') followed by 'باره' ('occasion/instance'), so 'نبد باره' = 'there was no (other) occasion/strike'. The conjunction 'و' links this to the next clause; 'مرد جنگی درست' is a nominal phrase ('the warlike man/hero proper') with an implied copula ('was' or 'remained') — i.e. 'and the warrior remained intact/steadfast.'
segments.s428-g19: 'همی' is an adverbial particle giving a continuous or emphatic past sense ('kept on / presently'), and 'تاخت' is the past of تاختن ('galloped/charged'). The prepositional phrase 'بر گردش اسفندیار' ('against/at the circuit/ranks of Esfandiyar') is the verb's object; word order is typical poetic/orderly: adverb + verb + prepositional object.
segments.s428-g20: This is a topicalized, somewhat compressed line: the plain, more regular word order would be 'تیرِ رستم به کار نیامد' ('Rostam's arrow did not come into use'). Here 'نیامد' (past negative of آمدن) is the main verb 'did not come/occur', and the displaced noun phrase 'برو تیر رستم به کار' expresses that Rostam's arrow 'did not take effect' or 'was not of use'. Poetic compression places the object phrase around the verb.
segments.s428-g21: 'فرود آمد' is a compound past meaning 'alighted / came down,' followed by the source 'از رخش' ('from Rakhsh'). The proper name 'رستم' appears after the prepositional phrase (poetic word order). The simile 'چو باد' ('like the wind') follows and modifies the verb, describing the manner of his descent.
segments.s428-g22: This clause is SOV-like: the object 'سر نامور' ('the famed head') comes first, then the directional phrase 'سوی بالا' ('toward/upwards'), and finally the past verb 'نهاد' (past of نهادن, 'placed/put' — here 'raised'). Together it reads 'he set/raised his famed head upward.'
segments.s428-g23: 'همان' ('that same') introduces the definite/identifying subject 'رخش رخشان' ('the radiant steed Rakhsh'). The verb 'شد' (past of شدن) with the directional 'سوی خانه' ('toward the house') gives 'turned/moved toward the house.' Adjective follows the noun in the phrase 'رخش رخشان' as is common in epic diction.
segments.s428-g24: 'چنین' ('thus/so') begins the result clause; 'با خداوند' ('with the lord/master') is the prepositional phrase, and 'بیگانه شد' is a two-word verbal phrase meaning 'became a stranger/alien.' The structure expresses a change of relation: 'thus he became a stranger to his lord.'
segments.s428-g41: سیه شد is a past-tense verbal phrase 'became/turned dark' (شد = 'became'). The patient/topic جهان ('the world') precedes the verb as is common in poetry; پیش چشمش is a prepositional/locative phrase 'before his eyes' with the possessive enclitic -ش. به رنگ introduces a description 'in the color/appearance of...' and links directly to the adjective/participle that follows in the next segment.
segments.s428-g42: خروشان is an adjectival/participial descriptor 'roaring, tumultuous' that completes 'به رنگ' from the previous segment; همی is an archaic/epic particle giving durative or emphatic aspect (here 'kept on / was') and precedes the past verb تاخت 'rushed/galloped'. تا introduces an extent 'as far as/until' and جای جنگ is a genitive-like compound 'place of battle'.
segments.s428-g43: تن مرد جنگی is a noun phrase 'the body of the war-man/warrior' (noun + modifying nouns). چنان modifies the adjective that follows, meaning 'so/such', and خسته is the predicate-adjective 'weary'. دید is the past transitive verb 'he saw' taking the preceding phrase as its object/topic; the order (object before verb) is regular in Persian and common in poetry.
segments.s428-g44: همه خستگیهاش is a possessive noun phrase 'all his fatigues/weariness' with the enclitic -ش 'his'. نابسته is an adjective here ('unbound, undone') applied to those fatigues, and دید repeats the verb 'he saw' (poetic repetition used for emphasis). The adjective precedes the verb, forming a compact nominal + adjective + verb sequence.
segments.s428-g45: بدو گفت is 'he said to him' with بدو a dative 'to him/unto him' and گفت (here used as the past tense verb 'said'); خیز is an imperative 'rise!' and اسپ من برنشین is an imperative clause 'mount my horse' (اسپ = 'steed/horse', من = 'my/I', برنشین = 'sit on/mount'). Imperatives appear in sequence as direct speech.
segments.s428-g46: که introduces a subordinate clause of purpose/result ('so that' / 'that'); پوشد is a 3rd-person form meaning 'cover/put on' (here optative/subjunctive in sense of purpose). ز بهر تو is a prepositional phrase 'for your sake' (ز poetic 'from/because of' + بهر 'for' + تو 'you'), and خفتان کین is a noun phrase 'the sleep of vengeance' (خفتان 'sleep' + کین 'hatred/vengeance'), the object/purpose of پوشد.
segments.s428-g47: بدو گفت again 'he said to him' (بدو = 'to him'). رو here is an imperative/command 'go/turn' (classical usage) and پیش دستان is 'before the dāstān(s)/storytellers' or 'in front of the tales' (پیش = 'before/in the presence of', دستان in this poetic context likely refers to tales/storytellers). بگوی is the imperative 'say/tell' (the verb بگو in poetic form). The sequence is a command: 'Go, tell before the storytellers...'.
segments.s428-g48: کزین = 'from this' (ک + ز + این fused, 'from this'); دوده 'smoke/soot' is the cause-agent phrase; سام is the proper name 'Sam' and شد is the past 'became/was turned'. رنگ و بوی 'color and scent' are the affected qualities. The clause literally says 'that from this smoke Sam's color and scent became (so)', i.e. they were changed by the smoke.
segments.s428-g25: Literal word order is poetic/inverted: the directional phrase 'به بالا' (upwards) and the source marker 'ز رستم' (from Rostam) come before the verb phrase 'همی رفت' and the noun 'خون' (blood). 'همی رفت' is an archaic/historic-continuous past form (همی + رفت) meaning 'was going' / 'flowed' — here describing blood moving upward; the noun 'خون' is the logical subject, placed after the verb for poetic rhythm.
segments.s428-g26: بشد is an older past/resultative of شدن meaning 'it became' or 'it happened (that)'; it takes 'سست و لرزان' (weak and trembling) as predicative adjectives describing the preceding 'خون'. The particle/word 'که' here introduces a following specification or comparison and links to the word 'بیستون' (Bisotun) — the exact idiomatic relation is poetic/elliptical, but the phrase conveys that the blood became weak/trembling in a manner evoking Bisotun (the mountain/place).
segments.s428-g27: بخندید is simple past 'laughed'. چر/چون here functions as a subordinating word 'as/when' ('when he saw him'). دیدش is the past-root دید plus the enclitic object pronoun -ش (him), so 'he saw him'. The subject 'اسفندیار' appears at the end for poetic word order: 'Esfandiyar laughed when he saw him.'
segments.s428-g28: بدو is a prepositional/enclitic sequence meaning 'to him' (به + او contracted), so بدو گفت = 'said to him'. کای is the vocative particle 'O' addressing someone. نامدار is an adjective 'renowned', modifying رستم. Note: گفت here is the 3rd person past of گفتن 'to say' (the provided sense list did not include a simple 'say' entry, so the best matching sense is not present).
segments.s428-g29: چرا is the interrogative 'why'. گم شد is a compound of the adjective/verb 'گم' (lost/vanished) plus شد (became), so 'became lost/vanished'. آن (that) modifies نیروی (the strength/force) and پیل مست is a noun phrase 'the (mighty) elephant, drunk/intoxicated' — a figurative description of the elephant's power. The whole segment asks why that power disappeared.
segments.s428-g30: ز پیکان = 'from/arising out of arrows' (ز as poetic 'from'). چرا again 'why' introduces the question. پیل جنگی is 'war/ battle-elephant' (پیل elephant, جنگی warlike/used in war). بخست is an archaic past of بستن (to close/shut); in this martial context it suggests the elephant was rendered useless/stopped up by arrows. The clause therefore asks why the war‑elephant succumbed because of arrows.
segments.s428-g31: کجا رفت = 'where went' / 'where has (he) gone'. آن مردی و گرز تو is a coordinated noun phrase ('that man and your mace') serving as the logical subject/complements of رفت; the verb precedes the full subject for poetic effect. The clause asks for the whereabouts/disappearance of both the man and the hero's weapon.
segments.s428-g32: به رزم = 'in/for battle' (به with a locative/Directional sense). اندرون here means 'within' or 'in the midst (of)'. فره (farr) is the traditional Persian concept of divine/noble glory or charisma, and برز means 'height' or 'exalted rank'; both are possessed by تو ('your'). The fragment rhetorically asks what has become of the hero's inner glory and high standing in battle.
segments.s428-g97: This clause has an inverted word order typical of classical Persian: بدو (to him) + گفت (said) + رستم (Rostam) = “Rostam said to him.” که introduces the reported speech/indirect question that follows. کنَم is the first‑person present-subjunctive/optative ('shall I do' / 'might I do'), so the following phrase is a question or deliberation directed by Rostam.
segments.s428-g98: چو here functions as 'when/if' introducing the condition or case under consideration. بر خستگیها = 'upon/against the weariness' (بر marks the target: the weariness). بر افسون کنم = 'I cast/should I cast a charm' (افسون = charm/spell; کنم is the subjunctive marker 'I should do'). The whole segment is the content of Rostam's question: 'when/if I cast a spell upon the weariness...'.
segments.s428-g99: چو برگشت = 'when he returned/turned away' (past). The prepositional phrase از رستم marks the source/point he turned away from. The proper name اسفندیار follows the clause as the subject in inverted, poetic order: 'When Esfandiyar turned away from Rostam...'.
segments.s428-g100: نگه کرد is a past tense compound (نگه + کرد) meaning 'he looked/kept watch.' تا here marks an extent/purpose ('until' / 'so as to see'). چون takes the sense 'how' ('in what manner'); رود in this context is the verb-form meaning 'he goes' (a subjunctive/optative sense in older Persian), so the clause means 'he watched to see how the famed one would go.' (Note: the provided sense list treats رود as a noun 'river' so its verbal use is not represented there.)
segments.s428-g101: چو بگذشت = 'as/when he passed' (past). مانند کشتی = 'like a ship' (simile). به رود = 'into/toward the river' (به marks direction). The whole phrase is a poetic simile describing the motion: 'as he passed like a ship into the river.'
segments.s428-g102: همی is an older emphatic/adverbial particle ('still/then/indeed') attached to the past verb داد ('gave'). تن را is the definite object with the object marker را. ز یزدان درود is a formulaic phrase meaning roughly 'to God / as a greeting/blessing from God' — in context idiomatically 'he gave up/entrusted his body to God.' The structure is simple past: 'he gave his body to God.'
segments.s428-g103: همی گفت = 'he kept saying' (present/frequentive sense in narrative). کای is a vocative marker 'O' addressing someone; داور is 'Judge' (here an epithet for God). داد and پاک are adjectives/nouns functioning as attributes: داد = 'just' (from 'justice'), پاک = 'pure'. The whole is an address: 'O just and pure Judge.'
segments.s428-g104: گر is the classical conditional 'if'. از خستگیها = 'from/out of weariness' (cause). شوم is first‑person present-subjunctive/optative 'I may become / I should become' (used here to express the possibility of perishing). من is the emphatic pronoun placed after the verb (poetic inversion). هلاک is the predicate meaning 'destroyed/ruined/perish(ed).' The clause reads: 'If from weariness I become destroyed.'
segments.s428-g145: چنین گفت پس = “thus/so he said; then”: چنين is an adverb 'thus/so' placed before گفت (past verb 'said'); پس is a temporal connector 'then'. با + name (با پشوتن) marks the addressee ('to/with Peshotan' — Persian uses با to indicate the person addressed). که here is the complementizer introducing reported speech (that...). Word order puts the reporting clause first and the reported clause after که.
segments.s428-g146: بپیچد is the present/potential form (prefixed بـ + verb) of پیچیدن, used here as third-person singular 'twist/turn (away)' in reported speech introduced by که. ز is the poetic preposition 'from', governing چنگال 'claw/talon'; مرد دلیر is the noun phrase 'the brave man' (مرد = man/hero, دلیر = brave) where دلیر is a postpositive adjective modifying مرد.
segments.s428-g147: به رستم نگه کردم امروز من: به رستم (to/at Rostam) is the prepositional phrase marking the target of the gaze. نگه کردن is a light verb construction (نگه + کردن) meaning 'to look/keep watch'; کردم is the 1st-person past 'I did' so 'I looked'. امروز من ('today I') is placed after the verb for emphasis — the subject pronoun من is optional and used here emphatically.
segments.s428-g148: بران is an adverbial/locative 'thereon/on that' linking to the place described next. برز (here 'height') + بالای ('above/on top of') form a compound locative 'high summit/height above', followed by آن پیلتن ('that Pilten') where آن is the demonstrative and پیلتن a place-name. The phrase as a whole means 'upon that high summit of Pilten.'
segments.s428-g149: ستایش گرفتم is a verbal phrase where the noun ستایش (praise) precedes the verb گرفتن (گرفتم, 'I took/did'), a common classical construction meaning 'I gave/expressed praise'. به یزدان پاک marks the beneficiary/recipient 'to the pure God'. The verb is past 1st-person (کردم/gرفتم).
segments.s428-g150: کزویست is an archaic fused form (کز وی است = 'from him is' / 'that is from him') introducing what proceeds as originating from God. The list 'امید و زو بیم و باک' enumerates nouns joined by و (and): امید 'hope', زو (ز + او) 'from him' + بیم 'fear' and باک 'dread/concern'. The preposition ز (in poetic form) marks source 'from'.
segments.s428-g151: که here introduces an explanatory clause ('for' / 'that'). پروردگار is the subject 'the Lord' and آن‌چنان is an adverb 'in such a way/so'; آفرید is the past 3rd-person 'created'. The clause states the manner of creation ('the Lord created thus').
segments.s428-g152: بران here resumes 'for that' / 'therefore' and introduces an address of praise: آفرین is used as the noun 'praise/acclamation'. کو is a classical relative/connector introducing the clause that follows (functioning like 'who/that' in older usage), and جهان آفرید is 'created the world' (جهان object + آفرید past verb). Together: 'praise to the Creator who created the world.'
segments.s428-g65: پشیمان شو is an imperative: 'be repentant' or 'repent' (پشیمان + شو). The coordinating و links a second imperative clause. In دست را ده به بند the object دست is made definite/emphatic by the object marker را and precedes the (understood) imperative verb ده 'give/put'; به بند means 'into fetters' (به = into/towards, بند = fetter). Word order is object (دست را) before verb (ده ... به بند) as is common in classical Persian.
segments.s428-g66: کزین پس is an adverbial phrase 'from this on / after this' placed before the clause. تو is the subject; از من is a prepositional phrase 'from me' marking the source; نیابی is the negative form of یابی ('you find' / 'you will find'), here meaning 'you will not find' or 'you shall not get'; گزند is the object 'harm'. The clause therefore means 'you will not find harm from me.'
segments.s428-g67: بدین خستگی is an adverbial phrase 'in this weariness / in this exhausted state' (بدین = 'with/ by this', خستگی = 'fatigue'). نزد شاهت is a prepositional phrase 'before/near your king' (نزد = 'in the presence of', شاهت = 'your king' with possessive -ت). برم is first-person singular (برد + م) used here as 'I will take/carry/bring (you)'; the subject (I) is implied.
segments.s428-g68: ز کردارها 'with respect to the deeds / concerning the deeds' (ز = 'concerning / from', کردارها = plural 'deeds'). بی‌گناهت is predicative '(you) innocent / blameless' with the second-person suffix -ت, meaning 'you shown/blamed-less'; برم repeats the verb 'I will take/present' so the full sense is 'I will present you before the king as innocent of your deeds.' Note the syntactic inversion where the predicate (بی‌گناهت) and verb follow the prepositional phrase.
segments.s428-g69: وگر introduces a conditional 'if (but if)'. جنگ جویی تو is 'you seek war/battle' (جویی here = seeking/search, تو = subject 'you'). اندرز کن is an imperative phrase 'take/seek counsel' or 'consult' (اندرز = advice, کن = do! i.e. 'take/do advice'). The clause is a conditional instruction: 'if you seek war, consult/take counsel.'
segments.s428-g70: یکی را is the object phrase 'someone / one (of them)' marked by را; نگهبان این مرز is the role/title 'guardian of this border' (نگهبان = guard, این مرز = this border). کن is the imperative 'make/appoint' (do). The construction 'یکی را نگهبان ... کن' = 'appoint someone as the guard of this border.'
segments.s428-g71: گناهی که کردی is a noun with a relative clause 'the sin that you committed' (گناهی = 'a sin', که کردی = 'that you did'). ز یزدان بخواه is an imperative phrase 'ask it of God' (ز = 'from/of', یزدان = 'God', بخواه = imperative 'ask/seek'). The verb is imperative directed to the addressee: 'ask God about the sin you committed.'
segments.s428-g72: سزد‌گر is a conditional/ concessive formula meaning 'if it is fitting / if (He) deems it proper' (implicitly referring to God as subject). به پوزش 'by/with a pardon' or 'on the basis of a plea for pardon' (به = 'by/for', پوزش = 'pardon/apology'). ببخشد گناه is 'he forgives the sin' (ببخشد = 'forgive' with the subject omitted/understood, گناه = direct object 'sin' placed after the verb in this poetical order). The whole means 'if He deems it fitting, He will forgive the sin upon a plea for pardon.'
segments.s428-g105: که is the interrogative 'who'; خواهد is the third‑person singular present/future of خواستن used here as 'will want/seek' (a future/intent reading). ز is the poetic preposition 'from' and introduces the agent/source (گردنکشان, 'the neck‑pullers' = rebels/proud ones). کین من ('my vengeance') is the object, marked by juxtaposition; literal order is 'who will want from the neck‑pullers my vengeance' (classical word order rather than modern English).
segments.s428-g106: که again functions as 'who'; گیرد is the present/optative form of گرفتن meaning 'seizes/takes' (here used as a future/rhetorical 'who will take'). The coordinated nouns دل و راه و آیین ('heart and way and custom') are the things taken, and من at the end is a possessive applying to all three (my heart, my way, my custom). The verb precedes its objects in the classical style.
segments.s428-g107: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'; اسفندیار is the subject. از پسش literally 'from/after him' (پس + pronominal suffix ـش) means 'after him' or 'looking behind him'. بنگرید is the past/epic form of نگریستن 'to look/gaze' (here 'he looked back').
segments.s428-g108: بران is an adverbial 'on/thereon/at that place'; روی here means 'surface/side' (i.e. that side or that flow's surface). رودش ('his river/flow') is a possessive compound (رود + ـش) and denotes a stream or figurative flow belonging to him; به خشکی 'onto the dry (land)' (به = towards/into; خشکی = dryness/dry ground). بدید is the past tense 'he saw'. The clause literally reads 'thereon the side/flow of his to dry ground he saw.'
segments.s428-g109: همی گفت is the iterative/continuous past 'he kept saying' or 'he said repeatedly' introducing speech. کین را is the definite object 'that vengeance' (را marks the object). مخوانید is from خواندن used here in the sense 'call/label' (imperative or exhortative plural/poetic present), so the phrase functions as direct speech: 'Call that vengeance a man.' مرد is a predicate nominative ('a man').
segments.s428-g110: یکی is an indefinite 'a certain'; ژنده is an adjective/noun meaning 'ragged' or 'tattered' (describing the person). پیلس is a rare/obscure word here naming that figure (left untranslated as a rare term). با introduces accompaniment 'with'; دار and برد are coordinated nouns ('دار' can mean abode/possession and 'برد' here 'gain/spoil' or 'victory') — together indicating the fellow's attributes. Word order is noun phrase followed by modifiers.
segments.s428-g111: گذر کرد is the past tense 'he passed/crossed'. پر خستگیها is a prepositional/qualifying phrase 'full of fatigues/weariness' (پر = 'full (of)'; خستگیها = plural 'wearinesses/fatigues'). بر آب is the locative 'over/on the water' (بر = over/upon; آب = water). The clause order places the verbal phrase after the adverbial and object, typical of classical style.
segments.s428-g112: ازان is the compound preposition 'from that/from it'; زخم پیکان is a genitive phrase 'wound of the arrow' (زخم + پیکان). شده is the perfect/participial form of شدن 'having been made/being made', and پرشتاب is an adjectival/advective compound meaning 'very swift/hasty' — together implying 'by/through that arrow‑wound he became (or was made) very swift/urgent'.
segments.s428-g137: Subject 'تو' (you) is fronted; 'کشتی' is the direct object (boat/ship). The prepositional phrase 'به آب اندر' (= into the water) precedes the past tense verb 'انداختی' (2nd person singular past) — a typical classical word order where the object and circumstantial phrase come before the verb. 'اندر' is an archaic/inverted locative meaning 'in/within' used with 'به' to yield 'into'.
segments.s428-g138: 'ز' here marks source/relationship ('from/of' or 'out of'), followed by the name 'رستم'. The particle 'همی' is an archaic emphatic/continuative particle (here an emphatic/attenuating 'indeed/just'), and 'چاکری ساختی' is a causative construction: 'ساختی' (you made) takes 'چاکری' (service/servitude) as its object — 'you made (him) into a servant.'
segments.s428-g139: The temporal subordinating conjunction 'چو' = 'when' introduces the clause. 'تابوت نوش‌آذر و مهرنوش' is a nominal phrase (poetic/genitive relation without an explicit ezafe) meaning 'the coffin(s) of Nush-Azar and Mehrnush.'
segments.s428-g140: 'ببینی' is the present/subjunctive form 'you see' used in the temporal clause ('when you see'). 'تو' is the explicit subject. 'در آز' is an adverbial phrase 'in (the grip of) greed.' 'چندین' ('many/a number of') modifies the omitted object(s), and 'مکوش' is a prohibitive negative imperative 'do not strive/attempt.' Together: 'When you see X, do not, from greed, attempt many things.'
segments.s428-g141: Word order is somewhat inverted/poetic: the locative phrase 'به چرم اندر' ('in/within leather') comes before the copula 'است' ('is'), then the noun phrase 'گاو اسفندیار' ('the ox/cow of Esfandiyar') follows. The construction states location ('is in leather') of Esfandiyar's ox.
segments.s428-g142: 'ندانم' = 'I do not know' (1st person present/past-used idiom). 'چه' = 'what' introduces the content of ignorance; 'راند' (past form) here means 'drives/has driven' (causal 'drive/force'), 'بدو' = 'to him/it' (dative), and 'روزگار' = 'fate/time' — so the phrase means 'I do not know what fate drives (it) to/into (that state).'
segments.s428-g143: 'نشست' here is the past verb 'sat' (form identical to the noun) meaning 'he sat down'. The phrase 'از بر تخت' is an older poetic locative meaning 'on/upon the throne'. 'با سوک و درد' is an instrumental/adverbial phrase 'with mourning and sorrow', describing his state as he sat.
segments.s428-g144: 'سخنهای رستم' = 'Rostam's words/sayings' (plural), with 'همه' = 'all'. 'یادکرد' here functions as a past verbal or verbalized noun meaning 'he remembered/called to mind' (classical compound 'یاد کرد'), so the clause reads 'he remembered all of Rostam's words.'
segments.s428-g81: ببندم is ب‑ (causative/imperative/subjunctive prefix in classical Persian) + بندم (1st‑person present-subjunctive/optative) and here functions as 'I will/let me close/put away'. همه خستگیهای خویش is a noun phrase: همه 'all' modifies خستگیهای (plural 'wearinesses, fatigues'); خویش + ـش (written here as separate خوی + ش) marks 'one's own' (possessive). Together: 'I will put away all my weariness.'
segments.s428-g82: بخوانم is the present-subjunctive/optative 1st person of خواندن with the b‑ prefix (here 'I will call/summon'); کسی را uses کسی 'someone' as definite/contrastive object marked by را. که introduces a relative clause 'who/that'; دارم is the present tense 'I have/possess' and به پیش is a prepositional phrase 'before (me)/in my presence'. The clause = 'I will call someone who I have before me.'
segments.s428-g83: This is a nominal list: زواره and فرامرز are personal names; و is the conjunction 'and'. دستان سام is a name phrase where دستان (here functioning as a name/epithet) is followed by سام (the proper name Sām). Word order is simply a poetic listing of persons.
segments.s428-g84: کسی را is 'someone' as a direct object (را marks the object). ز is the archaic/poetic preposition 'from'. خویشان 'relatives' is the noun governed by ز. که دارند نام is a relative clause 'who have a name' (که = 'who/that', دارند = present 'they have', نام = 'name' used here as 'reputation/fame'). Together: 'someone from the relatives who have a name (fame).'
segments.s428-g85: بسازم is the 1st‑person present‑subjunctive/optative of ساختن 'to make/do' with the b‑ prefix and here means 'I will accomplish/perform'. کنون 'now' is an adverb. هرچ 'whatever' modifies فرمان 'command/order'. تست in classical Persian here functions as an archaic clitic meaning 'is yours' (i.e. 'your'), not a modern noun — the provided sense list does not capture that function.
segments.s428-g86: همه راستی is a nominal phrase 'all (that) is true / all truthfulness' (همه modifying راستی). زیر پیمان is a prepositional phrase 'under the pledge/covenant'. تست again is the archaic copulative/clitic meaning 'is thine/your' (the sense lists given do not include this idiom), so the whole line says 'all truthfulness is under your covenant.'
segments.s428-g87: بدو is the prepositional phrase 'to him' (به + او contracted = بدو) marking the indirect object. گفت is the past tense 'said' (verb), here placed before the subject in poetic inversion. رویین‌تن اسفندیار is the subject 'the iron‑bodied Esfandiyār' (epithet + name). The given senses for گفت are noun senses and do not reflect the verbal past 'he said', so that sense is not available in the supplied list.
segments.s428-g88: که‌ای here is 'که' (introducing direct speech or a clause) plus ای (the vocative 'O'), functioning as an address 'O...'. برمنش is an epithet 'noble‑minded' or 'of fine disposition'; پیر is 'old' and ناسازگار 'uncooperative/incompatible'. Grammatically this is a vocative/pejorative phrase addressing the addressee: 'O noble‑minded old incompatible one' (ironically insulting).
segments.s428-g9: به رنگ طبرخون شدی این جهان is a predicative clause with poetic inversion: the adverbial phrase 'به رنگ طبرخون' ('in/with the color of طبرخون') comes before the verb 'شدی' (past of شدن, 'became'), and the subject 'این جهان' ('this world') is placed after the verb for emphasis. 'به' introduces the manner ('in the color of…'), 'رنگ طبرخون' is a noun phrase 'the color of طبرخون', and 'شدی' is the past/ perfective 'became' agreeing with the subject that follows.
segments.s428-g10: شدی آفتاب از نهیبش نهان has the same verb 'شدی' ('became') followed by the subject 'آفتاب' ('the sun') and the predicative adjective 'نهان' ('hidden'); 'از نهیبش' is a causal/prepositional phrase ('because of/from his نهیب' — his shout/roar) placed before the adjective. The structure thus reads literally 'became the sun from his shout hidden' — i.e. the sun was hidden by/at his thunderous cry. Note the adjective follows the causal phrase and the verb-subject order is a poetic inversion common in Ferdowsi.
segments.s428-g11: یکی چرخ را برکشید از شگاع is a transitive clause: 'یکی' ('one/a certain') quantifies 'چرخ' ('the sky/wheel'), which is marked by the object marker 'را'. 'برکشید' is the past-tense transitive verb 'lifted/raised', and the adverbial 'از شگاع' ('from/by (his) شگاع') gives the cause or manner — here 'شگاع' (ferocity/fierceness). The object-marking 'را' with a following verb and a final causal phrase is a typical word order in epic Persian.
segments.s428-g12: تو گفتی که خورشید شد در شراع is a reported clause. 'تو گفتی' literally 'you said' (past of گفتن) introduces the subordinate clause marked by 'که'. Inside it, 'خورشید' is the subject and 'شد در شراع' is the verbal phrase: 'شد' (became) + locative 'در شراع' ('in the sail' / 'in the banner'). Note that 'گفتی' here functions as the speech verb introducing reported speech; the internal clause again shows the poetic, inverted placement of verb and locative.
segments.s428-g13: به تیری که پیکانش الماس بود is an instrumental/associative phrase: 'به تیری' ('by/with an arrow'), followed by a relative clause 'که پیکانش الماس بود' ('whose point/shaft was diamond'). 'که' introduces the relative clause modifying 'تیری', and 'بود' is the past copula linking 'پیکانش' to 'الماس'.
segments.s428-g14: زره پیش او همچو قرطاس بود states a simile: 'زره' ('armor') is the subject, 'پیش او' places it 'before him/in his presence', 'همچو قرطاس' is the comparative phrase 'like paper/parchment', and 'بود' is the past copula 'was'. The order (subject — locative — simile — copula) reflects the poem's rhythm and emphasis.
segments.s428-g15: چو او از کمان تیر بگشاد شست begins with 'چو' ('when'), introducing a temporal clause. 'او' is the subject ('he'), 'از کمان تیر بگشاد' is a verbal phrase 'loosed an arrow from the bow' (بگشاد here = 'loosed/opened/released'). The final item 'شست' is an archaic/poetic form whose exact lexical force is uncertain in isolation (the lexicon entries give 'washed' or 'thumb'); in context it functions as part of the verbal/performative sequence and is best read as a poetic residue/emphatic past-form associated with the action of shooting.
segments.s428-g16: تن رستم و رخش جنگی بخست has the coordinated objects/affected parties 'تن رستم' ('Rostam's body') and 'رخش جنگی' ('the warlike steed Rakhsh'). The verb 'بخست' (past of بستن in a sense 'to close/tie') is used figuratively here in epic register to indicate that they were grievously struck/overcome/wounded — the physical-result reading fits the narrative. Word order puts the affected nouns before the verb for dramatic effect.
segments.s428-g153: چنین کارها رفت بر دست او is a compact clause: چنین ('such') modifies کارها ('deeds, affairs') which is the subject; رفت (past of رفتن, 'went/fell') is used here intransitively to mean 'came to / befell'; بر دست (literally 'on the hand') is idiomatic for 'into someone's control/charge' and او is the possessor 'his'. Word order is Subject–Verb–Prepositional phrase–Possessor (poetic order).
segments.s428-g154: که introduces an explanatory clause here ('for / because'); دریای چین is a metaphorical compound 'sea of folds/wrinkles' (دریای = sea + چین = folds); بود is the past copula 'was'; تا functions as a spatial extent 'as far as / up to'; شست here means 'thumb'; او is 'his'. The clause gives the reason or image explaining the preceding clause.
segments.s428-g155: همی is a habitual/continuous particle giving a repeated or emphatic past sense ('would / constantly'); برکشیدی is past (2nd person sing. or narrative past) 'you lifted up' (بر + کشیدی); ز introduces the source 'from'; دریا = 'the sea'; نهنگ = 'whale' (used literally or hyperbolically). The verb precedes the source in poetic order.
segments.s428-g156: به here functions instrumentally/locative ('by / with'), دم ('tip/edge' here used for 'tail') is the instrument ('by the tail'); در is 'door/gate'; کشیدی is again past 'you pulled/drew'; ز introduces origin 'from'; هامون = 'plain, flat expanse'; پلنگ = 'leopard/panther'. The sequence places the instrument and destination before the verb in a compact poetic order.
segments.s428-g157: بران سان is a fixed poetic phrase meaning 'in that manner / thus' (بران = 'upon that', سان = 'like/as'); بخستم is an archaic/polite past form used here (literally 'I gave' in an older sense) with the effect 'I yielded/gave up his body' to the weapon; تنش = تن + ش (body + his) is the definite object, and را marks it as the direct object; به تیر uses به instrumentally 'by/with an arrow'. The clause thus reads 'In that way I gave his body to the arrow.'
segments.s428-g158: که introduces the result clause ('so that / and'), از marks source 'from', خون او = 'his blood', خاک شد = 'became earth/ground' (شد = 'became'), and آبگیر = 'a pond / pooling of water'. Together the clause describes the consequence: from his blood the ground turned into a reservoir/pool.
segments.s428-g159: ز بالا = 'from above / from on high' (ز = 'from', بالا = 'up/high'); پیاده = 'on foot' (adverbial); به پیمان برفت treats به + پیمان as 'to the pledge' or 'to keep/perform the covenant' and برفت is an archaic past form of رفتن ('he went, set out') — here used poetically for 'he departed/advanced' to fulfill the pledge. Word order is adverbial phrase → manner → purpose → verb.
segments.s428-g160: سوی رود = 'toward the river' (سوی = toward(s), رود = river); با introduces accompaniment 'with'; گبر = 'gabr' (a Zoroastrian/infidel, here a person or epithet); و = 'and'; شمشیر = 'sword'; تفت is used adjectivally/epithetically ('scorching/fiery' or 'scorched'), from a verbal root meaning 'to burn/heat', so 'a scorching sword'. The phrase lists companions/items with which he advanced toward the river.
segments.s428-g121: سراپرده is a compound noun meaning the inner chamber/canopy (word order: head noun first). شاه‌پر here qualifies the canopy as royal (literally 'royal plume' used adjectivally), خاک means dust/ashes, and بود is the past copula 'was' — together: 'the inner canopy was dust-covered.'
segments.s428-g122: همه جامه = 'all the garments' (همه = all, جامه = robe/garment). مهتران is the plural 'mahterān' (chiefs/leaders) but that exact sense is not listed in the given options. چاک بود: چاک = torn/gash (adjectival), بود = past 'was' — 'all the chiefs' garments were torn.'
segments.s428-g123: فرود آمد is a two-word past action ('فرود' = descent/alighting, آمد = came) meaning 'he came down/descended.' از باره = 'on the occasion of / concerning' (از = from/of, باره = occasion), and اسفندیار is the proper name — 'he descended because of Esfandiyar's matter.'
segments.s428-g124: نهاد here is the past tense of نهادن 'to place/put' (sense 'placed'). آن = that, سر = head, سرکشان in the text means 'rebels' or 'insubordinates' but that exact sense isn't in the provided list, and برکنار = 'aside / to the side'. The clause literally says he 'placed that head of the rebels aside.'
segments.s428-g125: همی here is an iterative particle meaning 'kept (on) / still' and governs گفت (the past verb 'said' — note: the provided senses list for گفت is noun-like, but here it functions as the past of گفتن). زارا is an exclamation 'alas' or 'woe', and دو گرد جوان = 'two bands/companies of youths' (گرد as 'troop/group', جوان = young men).
segments.s428-g126: که introduces the subordinate content clause 'that/for'. جانتان = 'your life' (جان + possessive), شد is the past of شدن 'became' / 'left' (here 'has departed'), از = 'from', کالبد = 'the body', با = 'with', توان = 'strength/ability' — literally: 'that your life has become (departed) from the body with strength.'
segments.s428-g127: چنین گفت پس = 'thus he said then' (چنین = thus, گفت = said — here verbal despite the provided noun-like senses, پس = then). با پشوتن = 'with/to Peshotan' (با + proper name), که introduces direct speech, and خیز is the imperative 'rise!' or 'get up!'.
segments.s428-g128: برین is the union of بر + این ('on these/upon these'): بر = on/upon, ین = variant of این ('these'). کشتگان = 'the slain', آب = 'water' (literal here), چندین = 'several/much' and مریز is a negative imperative 'do not pour/spill'. So: 'do not pour much water upon these slain.'
segments.s428-g1: کمان برگرفتند و تیر خدنگ is a coordinated noun phrase + verb construction: برگرفتند is a past/plural verb 'they lifted/took up' whose direct object(s) are the coordinated nouns before and after و. کمان is the first object ('the bow'), و simply links the second object, and تیر خدنگ is a noun phrase where خدنگ is in apposition or adjectival to تیر ('a dagger/short-sword arrow' or 'dagger-like arrow'). The subject is omitted (they).
segments.s428-g2: ببردند is a past/plural verb 'they took/removed'; the source phrase is formed with the poetic preposition از (here written ببردند از) + روی خورشید 'the face/surface of the sun'. In this poetic word order the verb comes first, the source prepositional phrase follows, and رنگ (the object 'color') appears at the end — i.e. 'they took the color from the face of the sun.'
segments.s428-g3: ز here is the poetic form of از 'from' and takes the noun پیکان 'arrows' as its object. همی before the verb is a classical/Persian continuous or iterative marker ('kept on' / 'continually'), and افروختند is the past/plural 'they kindled' — so literally 'from the arrows they were continually kindling fire.'
segments.s428-g4: This line uses stacked locational particles: به + بر + بر ... yields a strong locative 'onto/upon the very surface of' (poetic emphasis by repetition). زره را is the definite object 'the armor' (را marks it), همی again marks iterative/continuous action, and دوختند is past/plural 'they sewed' — i.e. 'they kept sewing onto the surface of the armor.'
segments.s428-g5: دل شاه ایران is a genitive chain 'the heart of the Shah of Iran.' The following compound بدان is formed from ب‑ + آن (presented in the wordlist as separate elements) and functions as 'because of that / at that,' and تنگ شد is a past construction 'became narrow/constricted' (i.e. distressed). Grammatically this is Subject (دل شاه ایران) + circumstantial phrase (بدان) + predicate (تنگ شد).
segments.s428-g6: بروها here is an exhortative/imperative or vocative plural form ('go, away' or vocative addressing a group) and is followed by و 'and'. چهرش is 'his face/brow' (چهر + possessive -ش). پر is a predicative adjective 'full (of)', and آژنگ شد is the verb phrase where آژنگ (a poetic noun) is the content filling the face and شد is 'became' — so 'and his face became full of آژنگ.'
segments.s428-g7: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when.' او is the third-person subject 'he.' دست بردی is the verbal idiom 'دست بردن (به)' meaning 'to put/lay a hand (on)' or 'to reach for'; here بردی is a classical past form ('laid/put') used with او as subject. به سوی کمان is the directional phrase 'toward the bow.' The whole reads 'when he reached for the bow.'
segments.s428-g8: نرستی is the perfect/past sense 'was saved/escaped' (here used impersonally), and the inverted order نرستی کس means 'no one escaped' (کس here functioning as an indefinite/negative 'anyone/no one' in context). از تیر او is the source 'from his arrow.' بی‌گمان is an adverb 'undoubtedly/certainly' added for emphasis at the end.
segments.s428-g57: زَوَاره here is a proper name (a person) and stands first as the topical subject. ز is the poetic form of از meaning 'from' or 'away from', and پیش means 'in the presence of / before'. برادر is the noun 'brother'. برفت is an archaic past-tense verb-form (equivalent to رفت) 'he went/left' — the supplied lemma senses did not include this verbal sense.
segments.s428-g58: دو دیده is a direct object group 'two eyes'. سوی is the postposition 'toward'. رخش is the name of the steed (here 'Rakhsh'), and بنهاد is an older past-form meaning 'he set/placed/directed' (here 'directed his eyes'). تفت is an archaic/poetic perfect/preterite meaning 'he glared/turned (his gaze)'; that verbal sense does not appear in the provided senses list.
segments.s428-g59: به پستی is an adverbial phrase 'in lowliness / in a low posture'. همی بود combines the old epic imperfective particle همی with the past auxiliary بود to express continued or ongoing past state ('was being' / 'was'). اسفندیار is the subject name.
segments.s428-g60: خروشید is a past intransitive verb 'he cried out / roared'. کای here is the vocative particle 'O' (not the royal title), addressing رستم; نامدار is an epithet 'renowned' placed after the name.
segments.s428-g61: به بالا ('to/up on high') is an adverbial locution; چنین modifies manner 'thus/like that'. چند here means 'a little / for a short while'. باشی is a 2nd-person form meaning 'be / remain' (imperative/jussive sense 'stay'). به پای is an adverbial phrase 'at the foot' (literally 'to/at the foot').
segments.s428-g62: که introduces a subordinate clause 'for / because / that'. خواهد is the modal/future auxiliary 'will / is going to'. بدن is the noun 'body'. مر and ت together are first- and second-person clitics here (مر = 'me', ت = the 2nd-person clitic attached to the verb phrase or object), را is the object marker, and رهنمای is a noun 'guide' or 'one who will show/hand over' — the clause means roughly 'for he will give my body to you as a guide'.
segments.s428-g63: کمان is the direct object 'the bow'. بفگن is an archaic/poetic verb-form meaning something like 'seized/snatched' (this specific verbal sense is not in the provided senses). از دست is a prepositional phrase 'from the hand'. و is 'and'. ببر here is an imperative/hortative 'take!' or part of narrative compound; بیان in the text context names the thing taken (likely 'the reins' or similar), but the provided senses list does not match that meaning.
segments.s428-g64: برآهنج is a locative/expressive adverb meaning 'on the metal/armor/ breastplate' (literally 'on the iron'). و 'and' links clauses. بگشای is an older verbal form meaning 'he opened / he drew (out)' (here 'unsheathed'), تیغ is 'sword', and از میان 'from the middle' = 'from its sheath/within'.
segments.s428-g49: نگه کن is an imperative: نگه (literally 'gaze, watch') + کن (imperative 'do'), so 'look!'. که introduces the subordinate clause 'that/so that'. تا here links into the question clause and functions as a connective/emphatic particle leading into the interrogative 'what', not a temporal 'until' in this context. چاره کار چیست is a nominal phrase 'the remedy (چاره) of the affair (کار)' followed by the copula چیست ('what is'), so the clause asks 'what is the solution to the matter?'.
segments.s428-g50: برین خستگیها is a demonstrative phrase meaning 'these fatigues' (برین = 'these/these upon' + خستگیها = 'fatigues/wearinesses'); the following prepositional phrase بر آزار کیست asks the rhetorical question 'upon/to whom is [this] harm/affliction?'. کیست is the interrogative 'who is' (کی + ست). The two 'بر' tokens mark proximity/demonstrative and the relationship 'upon/against' respectively.
segments.s428-g51: که‌گر is an archaic/poetic conditional equivalent to modern اگر ('if'). The clause 'که‌گر من ز پیکان اسفندیار' literally gives the conditional protasis: 'if I, from Esfandiyar's arrows…' — ز here is the poetic/prepositional 'from/because of' and پیکان اسفندیار is the genitive construction 'Esfandiyar's arrows'.
segments.s428-g52: شبی را is an indefinite temporal object 'one night' (شبی) marked by را for emphasis/topic. سرآرم is a present/subjunctive/optative form 'I may rest/I could lay (my) head' — here used with the conditional meaning 'could rest'. بدین روزگار is a fused prepositional phrase 'in this time/age' (بدین = به + این 'to/at this', روزگار = 'time, life'). The whole segment is the apodosis of the preceding if-clause.
segments.s428-g53: چنان دانم = 'I think/consider it thus' (چنان 'thus/so' + دانم 'I deem/consider' — note دانم here is used in the sense 'consider' rather than mere factual 'know'). ای زال is a direct address 'O Zal'. کامروز من places time/emphasis: کامروز ('this very day/today') followed by the subject pronoun من 'I' for emphasis.
segments.s428-g54: ز مادر بزادم is the verbal statement 'I was born of (my) mother' where ز = 'from/of', مادر = 'mother', and بزادم is a classical form meaning 'I was born' (ب- prefix + زادم). بدین انجمن ('into this assembly/gathering') uses بدین (به + این) to mean 'into/into this' and انجمن = 'assembly/gathering'. The clause states the speaker's birth into the present company.
segments.s428-g55: چو رفتی is a temporal subordinate clause 'when you go/when you leave' (چو = 'when', رفتی = 2nd-person perfect/subjunctive used as 'you go/you leave'). همی is an archaic continuative/emphatic particle (here 'then/indeed'). چاره رخش ساز is an injunction: چاره رخش 'a remedy for Rakhsh' with ساز used imperatively 'make/prepare' (ساز is formally a noun but here functions as a verbal imperative 'prepare/make').
segments.s428-g56: من آیم is an optative/subjunctive 'may I come' or simple 'I will come' (آیم is present-subjunctive/optative of آمدن). کنون‌گر is a fused adverbial/particle ('now' or 'if now' in classical usage; here it situates the coming as immediate). بمانم دراز is a wish/request in the subjunctive 'let me remain long' (بمانم = 'I remain' subjunctive, دراز = 'long'). Together the clause expresses 'If I come now, may I remain for a long time.'
segments.s428-g33: گریزان is a participial adjective meaning "fleeing/being on the run" and functions adverbially here; به بالا is a prepositional phrase meaning "upwards/toward above;" چرا is the interrogative "why;" and برشدی is a 2nd-person singular past-form (بر + شدی) meaning "you rose/mounted". Word order is emphatic: adverbial participle + direction + question word + verb (you did X — why?).
segments.s428-g34: چو here means "when/as;" آواز is "voice/cry;" شیر is "lion;" ژیان is a poetic/older noun (here part of the lion phrase) — literally "life" in the dictionary but used as a modifier in the compound; بشندی is an older past form meaning "you sat/you took (a posture)" and is used here in a narrative/poetic sense ("when you heard/when the cry came, you (reacted)"). The whole segment gives the circumstantial time clause: "when the lion's cry came."
segments.s428-g35: چرا introduces a question "why;" پیل جنگی is a compound noun (پیل "elephant" modified by جنگی "warlike/used in war") meaning "war-elephant;" چو ... گشت is a simile/turning construction: چو "like/as" + روباه "a fox" + گشت (past of گشتن) "became/turned into." The verb گشت is 3rd-person past, describing the change.
segments.s428-g36: ز is the poetic preposition "from/because of;" رزمت is رزم + ت (the noun "battle/struggle" with the 2nd-person suffix -ت: "your battle/fighting"); چنین is an adverb "thus/so;" دست کوتاه گشت is an idiomatic result clause: دست "hand/power" + کوتاه "short" + گشت "became" meaning "your hand/power became diminished/short." The segment gives the cause and result: because of your fighting your power became so reduced.
segments.s428-g37: تو آنی که is a formula meaning "you are the one who..." (آنی functions as a substantive/adjectival element); دیو is the direct object "demon," and از تو marks cause/agent "because of you"; گریان شدی is a causative/resultive construction here: literally "made to be weeping" — i.e., "(you) caused the demon to weep." Grammatically, شدی is 2nd-person past used with the participle گریان to express that you brought that state about.
segments.s428-g38: دد here means "enemy/foe;" از marks origin/agent "by/from;" تف تیغ تو is a genitive phrase "the spit/blast of your sword" (تف = a spitting/blast, تیغ تو = your sword); بریان شدی is parallel in form to the previous clause: بریان ("roasted, seared") + شدی (2nd-person past) — i.e. "you caused the enemy to be seared" or more simply "an enemy was seared by your blade's blast." The verb form expresses action caused by the addressee.
segments.s428-g39: زواره is a proper name (Zovāreh); پی رخش is a phrase meaning "after/along (the) track of his steed" or "following his horse" (پی = after/in pursuit of, رخش = the horse's name/steed); ناگه is an adverb "suddenly," and بدید is the past tense "he saw." The clause is a sudden-perception narrative: "Zovāreh suddenly saw (his horse/its track)."
segments.s428-g40: کزان is a fused/poetic prepositional phrase meaning "from which/from that;" رود here functions as the narrative verb phrase (classical usage can present رَوْد/رود forms) or as a noun "stream/way" depending on context, but syntactically the phrase leads into the result clause; با خستگی is an adverbial phrase "with weariness," در is the locative/goal "into/inside," and کشید (past of کشیدن) means "drew/dragged (himself)" — together giving "from that he went and, weary, dragged himself inside." The clause describes his weary entry following what he saw.
